title: Manakish zaatar
subtitle: Lebanese flatbread topped with za'atar and olive oil
excerpt: A simple yeasted dough rolled thin and covered with a paste of za'atar spice blend and olive oil, then baked on a very hot surface until the edges puff and the topping forms a fragrant, slightly crunchy crust. Lebanon's most common breakfast — eaten folded, with tomato, cucumber, olives, or labneh, straight from the oven.
sourceNotes: Manakish (singular: man'oushe) are Lebanese flatbreads documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) as the standard Lebanese breakfast. The za'atar version is the most common. Za'atar is a Levantine spice blend of dried thyme, sumac, and sesame seeds. Manakish are traditionally baked on a saj (a convex iron griddle) but perform well on a baking tray in a very hot oven.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked shaped flatbreads on a tray, then bag. Bake from frozen at 240°C for 10 to 12 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate after shaping. Let it come to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

content.text: The za'atar paste is made by mixing the spice blend with enough olive oil to make it spreadable — about 3 parts za'atar to 2 parts oil. The paste should be thick enough to coat the dough without running off. Spread it generously almost to the edge of each flatbread, leaving a small border. It will look like too much; it is not. The za'atar cooks down and concentrates, and the oil soaks into the bread.

A very hot oven is essential. Place the baking trays inside the oven while it heats so they are scorching when the flatbreads go on. The dough should puff at the edges and the za'atar should sizzle and form a slightly firm crust in 8 to 10 minutes. Eat immediately — manakish are at their best within minutes of leaving the oven, when the edges are still crisp and the inside is soft and yielding.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} and 180 ml warm water. Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Divide into 4 equal pieces, roll into balls, cover with a damp cloth, and rest for 1 hour until slightly puffed.

Heat the oven to 240°C fan with two baking trays inside. Mix the {{za-atar}} with the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} to make a spreadable paste. On a lightly floured surface, roll each dough ball into a thin circle about 20 cm across. Spread a generous layer of za'atar paste over each, leaving a 1 cm border. Carefully slide onto the hot trays and bake for 8 to 10 minutes until the edges are puffed and golden.

items.symptom: Flatbread is thick and bready rather than thin and crisp
items.cause: Not rolled thin enough or oven not hot enough
items.fix: Roll to about 3 mm thick. The oven must be at full temperature before the bread goes in. Pre-heated trays help the base cook quickly

items.symptom: Za'atar topping is dry and crumbling after baking
items.cause: Not enough oil in the paste
items.fix: The paste should be loose enough to spread easily. Add more olive oil until it holds together and coats the bread in a generous layer

items.cause: Yeast not active or water too hot
items.fix: Fast-action yeast doesn't need proofing but water should be warm, not hot (no more than 40°C). If the dough feels dense and stiff after 1 hour, the yeast may have been killed — start again
Pre-heating the tray is essential for a crisp base. The bread should sizzle when it hits the hot metal

content.text: Manakish are the Lebanese equivalent of a morning pastry: quick to make or buy, eaten on the go, deeply satisfying. In Beirut, bakeries producing manakish on a large circular saj griddle are a neighbourhood institution: the flatbreads are sold by the piece, folded around olives, fresh mint, and tomato, and eaten standing at the counter or taken in paper to eat walking. The za'atar version is universal; a cheese version with akawi or halloumi is also common, as is a combination of the two. Za'atar itself varies by producer — some are heavy on sumac and sharp, others are more thyme-forward. The Levantine habit of dipping bread in olive oil then za'atar is the same flavour in a different form.
